coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedding CuCo-LDH hierarchical hollow structure for boosted charge storage capability in supercapacitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaojie
Xu, Dongbo
Ding, Jinrui
Zhou, Pengjie
Ying, Yulong
Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The nanostructure optimization of layered double hydroxide (LDH) can effectively alleviate fragile agglomerated problems. Herein, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) embedded in CuCo-LDH hierarchical hollow structure is synthesized by hydrothermal and impregnation methods. The electrochemical results show that the ordered multi-component structure could effectively inhibit the aggregation and layer stacking. At the same time, the hierarchical structure establishes new electron and ion transfer channels, greatly reducing the resistance of interlayer transport and accelerating the diffusion rate of electrolyte ions. Besides, NGQDs have both good electrical conductivity and abundant active sites, which can further improve the electron transmission rate and effectively strengthen the energy storage capacity of the material. Therefore, the large specific capacity of 1009 F g−1 can be displayed at 1 A g−1. The energy density of the assembled carbon cloth (CC)@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//activated carbon (AC) device can reach 58.6 Wh kg−1 at 850 W kg−1. Above test results indicate that CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC devices exhibit stable multi-component hierarchical structure and excellent electrical conductivity, which provides an effective strategy for enhancing the electrochemical characteristics of asymmetric supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Multi-component
Hierarchical hollow structure
NGQDs
CuCo-LDH

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Co–(Metal-Organic Framework) MOF on the carbon cloth
Preparation of CuCo-LDH on the surface of carbon cloth

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52203147 ) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LQ22B010006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram illustrating the synthetic method of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs.
SEM images of (a-c) CC@Co-MOF, (d-f) CC@CuCo-LDH and (g-i) CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs.
(a-b) TEM and HRTEM images of the CuCo-LDH/NGQDs. The element distribution mapping of (c) CuCo-LDH/NGQDs (d-h) Cu 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Co 2p.
(a) XRD patterns of CC@CuCoLDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs. (b-f) High-resolution C1s, Co 2p, Cu 2p, O 1 s, and N 1 s XPS spectra.

(a, b) The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and constant-current charge–discharge (GCD) curves of CC@Co-MOF, CC@CuCo-LDH, and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs were compared at 100 mV s−1 and at 1 A g−1. (c) CV curves (2–100 mV s−1) and (d) GCD curves (1–10 A g−1) of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs. (e) The calculated specific capacitance of CC@Co-MOF, CC@CuCo-LDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs. (f) Nyquist plots of three kinds of electrode materials.

(a) CV curves of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs at a scanning rate of 2 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1. (b) Linear relation graph of log(i) and log(v) of CV curves. (c) The ratio of capacitance contribution of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs electrode to total capacitance at scanning rates of 2, 5, 20, and 100 mV s−1. (d) Percentage of capacitance and diffusion control contribution of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs electrode.

(a) Model schematic diagram of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device. (b) CV distribution of positive and negative electrodes of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device in a three-electrode system. (c) Summary of CV curve of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device under 0–1.8 V voltage window. (d-f) CV curves, GCD curves and specific capacitance of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device.

(a) Ragone plots of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device and other reported ACS. (b) Cycle performance, Coulombic efficiency and practical application of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device (illustration shows that the device lights up 10 blue bulbs at the same time).
Performance comparison with other reported ACS.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedding CuCo-LDH hierarchical hollow structure for boosted charge storage capability in supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nanostructure optimization of layered double hydroxide (LDH) can effectively alleviate fragile agglomerated problems. Herein, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) embedded in CuCo-LDH hierarchical hollow structure is synthesized by hydrothermal and impregnation methods. The electrochemical results show that the ordered multi-component structure could effectively inhibit the aggregation and layer stacking. At the same time, the hierarchical structure establishes new electron and ion transfer channels, greatly reducing the resistance of interlayer transport and accelerating the diffusion rate of electrolyte ions. Besides, NGQDs have both good electrical conductivity and abundant active sites, which can further improve the electron transmission rate and effectively strengthen the energy storage capacity of the material. Therefore, the large specific capacity of 1009 F g−1 can be displayed at 1 A g−1. The energy density of the assembled carbon cloth (CC)@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//activated carbon (AC) device can reach 58.6 Wh kg−1 at 850 W kg−1. Above test results indicate that CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC devices exhibit stable multi-component hierarchical structure and excellent electrical conductivity, which provides an effective strategy for enhancing the electrochemical characteristics of asymmetric supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The existing reserves and fossil energy consumption can no longer meet the needs of long-term development in the future [1–4]. Meanwhile, the consequent environmental pollution problems also urge people to find a new way to harvest energy [5,6]. As one of the representative new energy storage devices, supercapacitors have shown great potential in power buffer applications, such as solar energy systems and smart power grids, due to their advantages of rapid charge and discharge, high power density, and excellent cycle stability [7–10]. However, compared with traditional batteries, the low energy density of supercapacitors has somewhat affected their practical applications [11]. Expanding the capacitance and voltage window range are two effective ways to increase the energy density [12,13]. Therefore, finding and selecting new high-capacity cathode materials can significantly optimize the potential window and become an effective way to construct high-energy–density asymmetric supercapacitors.

Layered double hydroxides (LDH) are often used as positive electrode materials in asymmetric supercapacitors because of their excellent layered structure, large specific surface area, and high theoretical specific capacitance [14–16]. However, the low conductivity and weak interlayer structure inhibit the comprehensive performance of LDH-based electrode materials in practical applications and lead to poor cycle stability and low multiplier ability [17]. The construction of binary transition metal hydroxide can maximize the synergistic effect of metal cations between layers, maintaining the advantages of LDH and further improving its electrochemical performance and cycling stability [18,19]. Among these transition metal hydroxides, Cu-Co hydroxide is a potential candidate because of its advantages of being environmentally friendly, low price, and a variety of oxidation valence states. On the other hand, nanostructure optimization is a reasonable and effective strategy by using the metal–organic framework (MOF) as the sacrifice template to grow the LDH material [20,21] and to form the layered structure through a multi-component combination [22,23]. It can provide a more stable morphological structure and establish ion transport channels, thereby significantly improving the electrochemical property and structural stability of materials. Cheng et al. [24] obtained Ni3S2/NiMn-LDH composite electrodes by controlling the curing time of NiMn-LDH. The sulfide on the surface is formed by vulcanization, which improves the conductivity of the composite electrode. The assembled supercapacitor device has a high energy density of 49 Wh kg−1 and a stabile capacity retention of 102 % after 10,000 cycles [24]. However, the traditional multi-component combination only enriched new materials on the surface, which may destroy the original interlayer structure and reduce structural stability. In addition, it is very important to explore the feasibility of introducing new component modifications and improving the interlayer structure of LDH.

N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) are a new zero-dimensional carbon-based material with small volume, large specific surface area, abundant functional groups, and excellent electrical conductivity [25,26], showing great potential in practical energy storage and arousing extensive research interests. NGQDs can be easily dispersed and embedded on LDH nanosheets, establishing interlayer channels between the nanosheets. Moreover, its surface has rich surface functional groups, providing more redox active sites [27]. It is worth noting that NGQDs can induce and change part of the electronic states and structures of the material, and the constructed electron-rich environment contributes to improving the electrical conductivity [28]. Therefore, constructing composite materials, i.e., loading NGQDs on LDH nanosheet, can enhance the structural integrity and cyclic stability of LDH material and directly and effectively obtain high-performance composite materials. However, it has not been widely used to improve supercapacitors' electrochemical performance.

This paper proposed a feasible in-situ preparation of LDH using Co-MOF nanorods as a sacrificial template. LDH nanorods were closely bonded with NGQDs by solution impregnation to form a hollow multi-component hierarchical layered structure. NGQDs loaded on LDH nanosheets can enhance the structural integrity and electrical conductivity and maintain the layered structure advantage of LDH. At the same time, due to their high conductivity and abundant active sites, NGQDs can effectively enhance the performance (capacitance and cycling stability) of the electrode material. After a series of tests, the CuCo-LDH/NGQDs-based electrode showed better capacitive performance (1009 F g−1) than the single-component CuCo-LDH (583.0 F g−1). In addition, the optimized assembled carbon cloth (CC)@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//activated carbon (AC) device exhibits a maximum energy density of 58.6 Wh kg−1 at a high power density of 850 W kg−1. Meanwhile, the device still maintains excellent cyclic stability under the high current density of 10 A g−1 (capacity retention rate of 85.7% after 10,000 cycles). Therefore, our work demonstrates that the introduction of NGQDs can improve the stability and capacity of the LDH material, expanding their application in supercapacitors.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O ≥ 98.0%), 2-Methylimidazole (C4H6N2 ≥ 99.0%), copper nitrate hexahydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O ≥ 99.0%), urea (CH4N2O ≥ 99.0%), ammonium citrate tribasic (C6H17N3O7 ≥ 98.0%), ethanol (C2H5OH ≥ 95.0%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH ≥ 98.0%) were provided by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. The above chemicals are of high purity and can be directly used in experiments. The carbon cloth used in the experiment was cut into several suitable sizes (1 cm × 2 cm) and 0.33 mm in thickness. The diameter of a single carbon fiber is 8.5 μm, which is generally made of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and braided after PAN fiber carbonization and vacuum baking. For nickel foam substrate, the porosity is 85 ∼ 98%, pore diameter is 5 ∼ 120 ppi, thickness is 0.5 ∼ 10 mm, and surface density is 280 ∼ 1500 g/m2.

The carbon cloths were treated with ultrasonic treatment in acetone, DI water, and ethanol solution. The treated carbon cloths have better hydrophilicity, which is beneficial to the growth of the surface electrode materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Co–(Metal-Organic Framework) MOF on the carbon cloth
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.58 g) and dimethylimidazole (1.32 g) were added into 40 mL of DI water, respectively [29,30]. Then above-prepared dimethylimidazole solution was quickly mixed into the Co(NO3)2·6H2O solution. At the same time, the processed carbon cloth was placed into the mixing solution and kept at 60 °C for 3 h. Finally, after 3 h, the prepared cloths decorated with Co-MOF were taken out, washed (alternately cleaning with ethanol and water), and dried (drying at 60 °C for 12 h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CuCo-LDH on the surface of carbon cloth
ce:section.ce:para: Cu(NO3)2·3H₂O (0.15 g) and urea (0.3 g) were dissolved in 14 mL of ethanol by sonication for 30 min [31,32]. The as-prepared Co-MOF@CC were immersed into the above solution and transferred to an autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction for 2 h at 120 °C. The resulting CuCo-LDH@CC was repeatedly cleaned with ethanol and water, vacuum-dried, and used for the next experimental operation.

The preparation method of NGQDs is as follows: 1 g of ammonium citrate tribasic was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water and then heated in the oil bath to 200 °C. After 30 min, the solution turned light orange. Then the appropriate amount of sodium hydroxide solution (10 mg mL−1) was added to the solution to neutralize the pH, and NGQDs were prepared [33]. The CuCo-LDH material was immersed in the NGQDs dispersion (diluted 10 times). After 15 min, the as-prepared CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs were washed repeatedly with ethanol and water and dried in the oven for 12 h.

Using SEM (S-4800 from Hitachi, Japan) and TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopy) is able to identify the morphology and microstructure of the material surface,. XRD (D/MAX-2500 diffractometer, with Cu CuKα radiation) can be applied to judge and analyze the crystal surface distribution in the material. XPS (ESCALAB 250Xi Gamma Ray Spectrometer, 150WAl Kα) was employed to determine the composition of the elements contained in the material.

To further investigate the behavior of composite electrode materials, there is a need to build a three-electrode system to employ the counter electrode (Pt electrode), the reference electrode (Hg/HgO electrode) and the working electrode (the prepared positive electrode material). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve, constant-current charge–discharge (GCD) curve and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test can be obtained to characterize the composite electrode material by CHI 760E workstation in 6 M KOH solution. In the lightning experiments, two CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQD//AC devices in series are charged for 10 s to light 10 blue bulbs in a voltage of 0–3.4 V.

Fig. 1 indicates the preparation process of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs material. The treated carbon cloth (CC) was first immersed in the dimethyl imidazole colloid solution to grow rod-like Co-MOFs. The Co-MOFs were then transformed into CuCo-LDH nanosheets in-situ on the surface by hydrothermal reaction. Finally, the NGQDs were uniformly adhered to the CuCo-LDH nanosheets through static interaction, resulting in the hierarchical structure with internal hollow CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is applied to characterize material surface morphology and composition. Fig. 2 a-c shows the morphologies of the Co-MOF nanorod array on carbon cloth from the whole to the local. The nanorods (about 0.36 µm in length) are well-scattered and evenly distributed on the surface. The longitudinal growth of Co-MOF nanorods array provides a skeleton for constructing self-supporting electrodes and can be used as a robust template for fixing CuCo-LDH nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 2d-f, CuCo-LDH nanosheets are successfully grown on the nanorods with a length of about 0.45 µm through hydrothermal reactions. After etching through the action of hydrothermal reaction, the one-dimensional nanorod structural Co-MOF was observed to transform into a layered nanosheet structure of CuCo-LDH with higher specific surface area and richer active centers. Because of the neatly arranged nanosheets on the surface and the stable internal skeleton, the layered structure of CC@CuCo-LDH helps increase the amount of electron and electrolyte ion transporting, greatly enhancing the charge storage ability of the electrode material. To further construct multi-component hierarchical electrode materials and optimize the electrode performance, NGQDs are loaded on the nanosheet at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 2g-i, slight morphology changes were observed after immersion in NGQDs aqueous solution, where the top end of the nanorod opened while the decorated nanosheets were still stably and orderly arranged on the surface. The hollow structure can supply a larger effective SSA and extensive active sites to expand the contact between electrode and ions. NGQDs have a broad specific area and rich oxygen-containing functional groups, providing abundant active sites for enhancing electrochemical performance. The TEM images of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs in Fig. 3 a showed that the surface of the bladed CuCo-LDH nanosheet was crisscrossed with dark and light, indicating the successful loading of NGQDs on the CuCo-LDH nanosheet surface. Densely distributed NGQDs with clear lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, well matched with the crystal plane (100) of NGQDs [34], were obtained and displayed on the HRTEM image in Fig. 3b. It proved that the introduced NGQDs are closely connected to the surface of CC@CuCo-LDH, forming a hierarchical structure. In addition, the lattice fringes of 0.150 nm, 0.154 nm, and 0.158 nm were attributed to the (003), (110), and (111) planes of CuCo-LDH, respectively [31]. In addition, Fig. 3c-h records the uniform distribution of each element (Cu, C, N, O, and Co) among the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs material.

XRD is applied to further study the crystalline phase and purity of the electrodes (Fig. 4 a). In the XRD patterns of CC@CuCo-LDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs, except for two peaks at 2θ = 26.1 and 43.3° belonging to CC, all the rest peaks (2θ = 13.1, 21.9, 34.1, 36.6 and 39.2°) are consistent with the characteristic peaks of CuCo-LDH (JCPDS No. 84–0599), which preliminarily proves the successful preparation of the electrode material [31,35]. No additional peak of NGQDs was observed in the XRD pattern of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs due to the low-loading content. The XRD characterization was well matched with the TEM lattice fringe information in Fig. 4a, demonstrating that NGQDs can keep the integrity of the original structure of CuCo-LDH, alleviate the instability of crystal structure and successfully construct a stable multi-component layered nanosheet structure. The elemental valence state of the CuCo-LDH and CuCo-LDH/ NGQDs were investigated by XPS. There are three significant peaks in the C 1 s spectra of CC@CuCo-LDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs at 284.8, 285.7, and 288.4 eV, standing for a carbon-hydrogen bond, carbon–oxygen double bond and carbon atom in turn (Fig. 4b) [36]. A total of six peaks were obtained by fitting the Co 2p spectrum in Fig. 4c, where two satellite peaks (786.9 and 803.3 eV), two Co 2p3/2 peaks at the binding energy of 781.3 and 797.4 eV, and two Co 2p1/2 peaks at 783.3 and 798.7 eV were observed [37]. Two characteristic peaks of Co 2p move forward, namely, the increase of binding energy. The strong interaction between the electrons of NGQDs and CC@CuCo-LDH may contribute to the positive shift of the Co 2p binding energy, which can effectively improve the rate performance of electron transport in the electrode. Furthermore, the peak intensities corresponding to Co2+ and Co3+ are reduced slightly, indicating the co-existence of Co2+ and Co3+ at CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs, which would contribute to the material's ability to store charge capacity. In Fig. 4d, the Cu 2p3/2 XPS spectrum has two characteristic peaks at 934.5 and 935.5 eV, while Cu 2p1/2 has two characteristic peaks at the binding energy of 954.0 eV and 955.9 eV. The satellite peak is located at 943.0 and 962.8 eV [37]. It can be found that Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 peaks positions slightly shifted, and their peak areas are reduced compared with CC@CuCo-LDH without the introduction of NGQDs, which indicates that NGQDs changes the electron structure in CC@CuCo-LDH and thus can affect the electron transfer velocity. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4e) has three obvious peaks at 530.5, 531.4, and 532.0 eV, which match with M−O−M, MOH, and HOH bonds, respectively [38]. The MOH peak height in CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs is higher than before. However, there are no other obvious changes, indicating that only part of the electronic environment in O atoms is affected. Three types of N species, i.e., pyridone N, pyrrolidine N, and graphitic N, were fitted from the N 1 s region of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs (Fig. 4f) at 399.1, 400.1, and 401.5 eV, respectively [27]. It is reported that pyrrole nitrogen can provide richer active centers and higher electrical conductivity, and the presence of graphene N could effectively improve electrical conductivity and hydrophilicity [27]. In this work, the proportion of pyrrole nitrogen is higher than that of the other two. Therefore, it is predicted that the conductivity of CC@CuCo-LDH can be effectively enhanced by adsorbing quantum dots on the surface of nanosheets.

Fig. 5 a presents the CV curves of three working electrodes in a three-electrode system at a scanning rate of 100 mV s−1. Obvious redox peaks can be observed in both CC@CuCo-LDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs. The possible Faraday reaction equations are as follows [29]:
Cu(OH)2 + OH–↔CuOOH + H2O + e-(1)
Co(OH)2 + OH–↔CoOOH + H2O + e-(2)
CoOOH + OH–↔CoO2 + H2O + e-(3)

Moreover, the integral area of CC@CuCo-LDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs is much larger than that of pure Co-MOF, indicating the greatly improved electrochemical property. In addition, the introduction of NGQDs leads to a significant increase in electronic conductivity according to the subsequent GCD test. The GCD curves were employed further to evaluate the more detailed electrochemical properties of those three electrodes. As expected, the capacitance of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs material (1009.0 F g−1) was obtained by substituting the measured results into the formula from Fig. 5b. In comparison, the obtained capacity of CC@CuCo-LDH is only 583.0 F g−1 and the Co-MOF is 117.4F g−1. The CV curves of CC@CuCo-LDH and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs have obvious charge and discharge platforms, which corresponds well to Fig. 5a. As can be noticed from Fig. 5c, the shape of the redox peak remains the same despite the continuous increase in the scanning rate. However, the two redox peaks move slightly in the positive and negative directions due to the polarization caused by internal resistance. When the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs electrode is given different current densities (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, and 10 A g−1, respectively), the corresponding specific capacities of the electrodes are 1009.0, 971.6, 711.0, 613.0, 497.6 and 436.0 F g−1 in Fig. 5d. Besides, the tested electrochemical performance of CC@Co-MOF, CC@CuCo-LDH, and activated carbon electrodes is displayed in Fig. S1-2. From the specific capacitance comparison in Fig. 5e, the capacity curve of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs is always above the other two, indicating that the electrochemical capacitance of the electrode material can be effectively enhanced by introducing quantum dots. We have carried out electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests on CC@Co-MOF, CC@CuCo-LDH, and CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs, and obtained Nyquist curves that can give an in-depth understanding of the electron transmitting capacity of electrode materials and the mechanism of electron and ion diffusion. A typical Nyquist curve comprises a semicircle and straight line (frequency range: 0.01–100 kHz). The X-axis intersection distance obtained in the high-frequency region corresponds to the equivalent circuit, representing the equivalent series resistance (Rs), i.e., the resistance contained in the electrode material itself. The diameter of the semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), relating to how fast the charge moves [39–41]. The CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs make the smallest Rs and Rct values, the CC@CuCo-LDH does more, and the Co-MOF does the biggest because the layered structure of Co-MOF-derived CuCo-LDH has a higher specific surface area (SSA) and more diverse ion diffusion and transfer pathways. At the same time, the reasonable distribution of NGQDs on CuCo-LDH nanosheets provides more active centers, accelerates the transfer rate of electrons and ions, and further improve the electron transmitting capacity of the electrode.

In general, the reaction kinetics of the electrode material is assessed by calculating the capacitance efficiency from the test results of the CV curve and by the formula i = avb, where the measured current (i) and scanning rate (v) are obtained from the CV curve in Fig. 6 a [42–44]. After logarithmic calculation of the two, linear equations fitting the anodic peaks and cathodic peaks of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs are obtained, respectively. The value b in the equation is the slope of the linear equation. Therefore, the electrochemical kinetics of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs material can be explained by the value of b. Generally, the processes of surface capacitance and diffusion control work in tandem to manage the electrode's energy storage process. (b = 0.5 ∼ 1). If b = 0.5, it is determined by the diffusion control process, while if b = 1, the surface capacitance process dominates the behavior [45]. Fig. 6b shows that the slopes of anodic and cathodic peaks are 0.59 and 0.51, respectively. Therefore, with b values of 0.59 and 0.51, the surface capacitance process and diffusion control process in CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs material jointly affect its energy storage capacity can be included. In addition, to further explore the electrochemical properties of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs electrode material, The formula below can be used to compute the ratio of the contribution of the diffusion control process to the capacitance control process: i(V) = k1v + k2v1/2, where k1v represents that the capacitor control part plays a dominant role in the total process and k2v1/2 indicates that the diffusion control part contributes more [46–48]. In Fig. 6c, the ratio of the red area to the green area can be clearly expressed as the percentage of capacitor control contribution. The proportion of capacitance control contribution is relatively small (54.5 %) at a relatively small sweep rate (2 mV s−1). However, with the gradual increase of the scanning rate to 100 mV s−1, the proportion of capacitor control contribution also increases significantly, reaching the maximum of 80.34 %, indicating that quantum dots are conducive to enhancing the conductivity of electrons and increasing the rated capacity.The percentage contribution of capacitance and diffusion control to the total process in CuCo-LDH/NGQDs at the same scanning rate is shown in Fig. 6d in more detail. It is shown that the contribution percentage of capacitance control increases from 54.5 % to 80.34 %, and correspondingly, the diffusion control percentage decreases from 45.5 % to 19.66 % with the increase in scanning rate.

To evaluate the comprehensive capability of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs electrode materials in practical application, CC@AC was selected as the negative electrode, the electrode material was used as the positive electrode, the electrolyte was 6 M KOH, and the corresponding ASC device was assembled in Fig. 7 a. Fig. 7b shows CV curves of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs and CC@AC. The redox peak of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs is found to be at 0–0.5 V, and the CV curve of CC@AC shows a rectangle at −0.5–0 V. Other test results of CC@AC at the three electrodes are presented in Fig. S2. The voltage window of the assembled CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device can reach 1.6 V. There is no obvious deformation of the CV curve under the 1.6 V voltage window (Fig. 7c). In contrast, the curve deviations and polarization occur at 1.7 V. Subsequently, the CV curve of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC is fixed in the voltage window of 0–1.6 V, as shown in Fig. 7d. All CV curves are similar in shape, indicating good reversibility even at high scan rates. The GCD curve of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC is shown in Fig. 7d and 7f. By calculation, the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC asymmetric supercapacitors at 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, and 10 A g−1 can be obtained with a specific capacitance of 145.2, 129.5, 124.7, 115.9, 108.6, and 106.2 F g−1, respectively. The assembled CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC still maintains high capacitance under high current density with increased current density.

The measured GCD diagram determines the correlation between energy density and power density in order to better assess the energy storage capacity of supercapacitors in practical applications. The Ragone diagram of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC can be obtained (Fig. 8 a). The outstanding energy density of CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC devices, which is 58.6 Wh kg−1 at 850 W kg−1, surpasses that of many asymmetric supercapacitors containing copper, cobalt, or quantum dots in Table 1 . The remarkable electrochemical performance is due to the synergistic effect between NGQDs and CuCo-LDH nanosheets, which promotes the rapid diffusion of electrons and ions between layers and makes the multi-component hierarchical structure with high conductivity. The capacitance retention rate of the CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC device (in Fig. 8b) is 85.7 % after 10,000 charge–discharge cycles. After 10,000 cycles, the Coulombic efficiency still maintains 99.8%, indicating the good reversibility of electrode materials. In addition, the use of two CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQDs//AC devices in series in Fig. 8b (with CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQD as positive and AC as negative) can simultaneously light up 10 blue bulbs for 61 s, demonstrating great potential as an energy storage device for practical applications.

In summary, the CC@CuCo-LDH/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) composite with hollow inner multi-component hierarchical structure was obtained by introducing NGQDs into the surface of CuCo-LDH nanosheets. The microstructure, elemental composition, and electrochemical performance were carried out. It is demonstrated that the introduction of NGQDs can construct interlayer ion and charge channels and improve the migration rate of ions and charges, resulting in excellent capacitance, high energy density, and great cycle stability of 85.7 % after 10,000 cycles. It is important to mention that the NGQDs loading procedure is prepared and capable of swiftly and effectively enhancing the material's electrochemical performance. This method can be applied to other electrode materials preloading with NGQDs, and improve their morphology and structure. Therefore, it is extremely great as a reference for designing and developing high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52203147) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LQ22B010006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.06.106.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sun, Q. Fang, Z. Li, C. Cai, H. Li, B. Cao, W. Shen, T.X. Liu, Y. Fu, Co-precipitation synthesis of CuCo2O4 nanoparticles for supercapacitor electrodes with large specific capacity and high rate capability, Electrochim. Acta 397 (2021) 139306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational synthesis of Cu7Se4-CuxCo1-xSe2 double-shell hollow nanospheres for high performance supercapacitor
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, X. Chen, H. Cao, C. Li, L. Wang, Y. Wu, C. Wang, Y. Li, Rational synthesis of Cu7Se4-CuxCo1-xSe2 double-shell hollow nanospheres for high performance supercapacitor, J. Power Sources 480 (2020) 228741.
